title: Nathanial's Season of Disruption: Three DNFs, Zero Points, and a Teammate Pulling Away
meta: Published: 01 April 2026 | By Marcus “Stats” Chen, Data & Performance Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Joshua Nathanial has yet to score a point in 2026 — while teammate Zhao Lei has 23.
body:
Four races into the 2026 season, the McLaren garage houses two drivers operating in entirely different championships. On one side, Zhao Lei has quietly accumulated 23 points through four point‑scoring finishes, placing him eighth in the drivers' standings and giving McLaren ninth place in the constructors' fight. On the other side, Joshua Nathanial has zero points. He has not finished three of the four events he has entered — two grand prix retirements and a sprint DNF — and the only race he completed, the Chinese Grand Prix, he finished a lap down in 17th after opening‑lap damage made his car all but undriveable.

The contrast between the two McLaren drivers is not a reflection of pace. Nathanial qualified third in Japan, his best Saturday of the season, placing himself firmly in the fight for a podium. Lei has reached Q3 only once. Yet Lei has points on the board in every grand prix: P7 in Australia, P8 in the China sprint, P4 at Shanghai, and P8 at Suzuka. Nathanial has nothing. The gap — 23 points to zero — is already the largest intra‑team disparity on the grid, and it has been built almost entirely on circumstance rather than performance.

The DNFs: A Pattern of Wrong Place, Wrong Time

Nathanial's three retirements share a common thread: none were his fault. In Australia, he was eliminated on the opening lap under a virtual safety car, spinning at the same treacherous corner that claimed Julian Kondra's Alpine. In the Shanghai sprint, a first‑lap midfield collision involving Robin Keisner left his McLaren with terminal damage. In Japan, after a brilliant qualifying performance that placed him P3 on the grid, he was running in a points‑paying position on lap 38 when Maikel Zwart's Mercedes slammed into the back of his car under braking. Nathanial had done nothing wrong; he was simply collected. The result was the same: DNF, zero points, a long walk back to the paddock.

The statistical weight of those retirements is crushing. Nathanial has completed just 78 racing laps across four weekends. Lei has completed 209. The Australian's longest run of uninterrupted laps came during the Chinese Grand Prix, where he circulated a damaged car for the entire race distance, finishing a full lap behind the leaders. That performance, though gritty, earned him no points — only a footnote in the results sheet.

The Teammate Comparison

Lei has been the model of consistency. His season has been built not on headline performances but on relentless accumulation: a P7 in Australia, a single point from the China sprint, a P4 at Shanghai that delighted the home crowd, and a P8 at Suzuka. He has extracted points from every weekend, regardless of conditions or strategy. His qualifying has been solid but unspectacular — he has yet to qualify higher than fifth — but his racecraft has been faultless. He has not made a single error that cost a result.

Nathanial, by contrast, has shown flashes of brilliance — his Japan qualifying lap was three‑tenths quicker than Lei's best Q2 time — but has been unable to convert that speed into Sunday results. The gap between the two drivers is not talent. It is luck, or the absence of it. And in a sport where points are the only currency, that absence is beginning to define Nathanial's season.

Australian GPP7 (6)DNF (0)
China SprintP8 (1)DNF (0)
Chinese GPP4 (12)P17 (0)
Japanese GPP8 (4)DNF (0)
Total230

McLaren's constructors' position — ninth, with 23 points, a single point behind Aston Martin and four behind Red Bull — is almost entirely the work of one driver. The team cannot afford to carry a zero‑scoring car for much longer. Nathanial's pace is not in question. His fortune is. And with Bahrain next, a track where the team expects the Mercedes engine upgrade to pay dividends, the pressure to break the cycle is immense. The season must begin. For Nathanial, it still hasn't.

1 Nathanial has been classified as a finisher in only one grand prix this season. Lei has been classified in all four. The gap in completed race distance: Nathanial 47% of total laps; Lei 100%.
